title: Born in Sasser in 1940
summary: Interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries at Albany State University in March 2013, Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar opens with her birth in Sasser, in Terrell County, Georgia, on February 4, 1940, to Clifford and Johnnie Lou Brawner, and traces her father's people from Worth County and her mother's roots back to Alabama.

title: I Didn't Like the Farm
summary: McCullar describes her hardworking father, who farmed, worked at the Marine base, and retired after about 28 years at the Swift fertilizer plant in Albany, and her own dislike of farm life near Doublegate on Old Dawson Road, the animals, heat, gnats, and snakes she feared, and the move off the farm to Sasser when she was twelve or thirteen.

part: Schooling in the Jim Crow South
title: A One-Room School Called Piney Grove
summary: She recalls a school year that ran October to April so children could work the rest of the time, the one-room Piney Grove school of seven grades, one teacher, and about forty students, her first teacher Vanny Wakefield, and her later moves to Dunbar in Sasser and to Carver High School in Dawson.

part: Schooling in the Jim Crow South
title: Books With the Pages Torn Out
summary: McCullar describes Carver High School in the mid-1950s, cold rooms with one heater and no lunchroom, a partition between two classrooms doubling as an auditorium, and the hand-me-down books from white schools with pages torn out, sometimes one spelling book per class, conditions her parents knew left Black children improperly educated.

part: Albany State and a First March
title: Waiting a Year for Her Sister
summary: After graduating in 1959, McCullar stayed home a year keeping house and babysitting while her older sister finished nursing school in Bibb County near Macon, then chose Albany State so her sickly self would not be far from home, with her parents, who wanted her to teach or nurse, deciding her path.

part: Albany State and a First March
title: Hepatitis From the Food
summary: Enrolling at Albany State in 1960 with a vague plan to study science, McCullar enjoyed living on campus but found the food so badly prepared that she and other students complained to an unresponsive administration, and she came to believe the food made her ill.

part: Albany State and a First March
title: A March Downtown With Bertha Gober
summary: McCullar recounts her first and only march at Albany State, a downtown demonstration she connects to a young woman named Bertha Gober, the fear of not knowing what would happen, and how her parents, learning of it from a white head cook who told her mother the girl should have known better, were not angry because things were opening up.

title: Becoming Secretary of the Terrell Movement
summary: Falling ill in spring 1962 and returning home to recuperate, McCullar found her parents already going to meetings of the Terrell County movement, which had begun around 1960, held first at a Baptist church in Dawson and then in the family home, and somehow she was drawn in as secretary under its president, Lucius Holloway.

title: My Mother Lost Her Job for Registering
summary: McCullar describes the family's efforts to register at the courthouse basement, where the registrar, who also worked at the post office, would leave to avoid them and made applicants read a Bible scripture, and how after they registered her mother was fired from the white school and a later employer in Albany refused to fire her.

title: Sheriff Mathews at the Mass Meetings
summary: As secretary she took notes as people described what they faced on their jobs and SNCC workers like Charles Sherrod, Don Stewart, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen spoke, while Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies would come to the Mount Olive meetings, put their feet on the seats, and insist outsiders were stirring up contented Black people.

title: Always Around the County
summary: McCullar recalls meeting Charles Sherrod, whom she found a unique, soft-spoken, self-assured person, the SNCC workers' constant visibility in the county, and where they stayed, a woman named Carolyn Daniels housing some and Lucius Holloway housing others, while her mother made cakes to feed them.

title: They Called Us Freedom Riders
summary: With high school students Gladys Clark and her sister Anadine Clark, McCullar canvassed Dawson after school, met people who ran inside or were too afraid of being fired or evicted to talk, walked or hitchhiked home until dark, and always went in pairs or threes for safety as cars swerved at them on the road.

title: Just Sit at the Counter
summary: McCullar describes occasional sit-ins with Reverend Samuel Wells at a restaurant in Dawson, where he instructed them only to sit at the counter and leave quietly if confronted so no one would be hurt, and how patrons and owners would call them names and refuse service while they simply got up and left.

title: Teaching the Doves to Sign Their Names
summary: After the church burning, McCullar attended an adult literacy school that taught people who could not read or write to sign their names, recalling students like Catherine Mallory rejoicing at learning their alphabet, and she served as a poll watcher in local and national elections, helping voters who needed it choose their candidates.

title: Like a Death in the Family
summary: McCullar describes the August 1962 burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church, the family church where mass meetings were held, as feeling like the death of someone loved, a blow that only made the people more determined, after which meetings moved briefly to a Methodist church and then to a tent set up behind a foundation her brother helped build.

title: Taking Turns to Watch the House
summary: With the word out that movement participants' homes might be burned, McCullar's family took turns staying up at night watching for suspicious or strange cars, ready to get everyone out before a fire could be set, and she notes that one uncle, her father's brother, was also active while most relatives and returning classmates were not.

title: They Took My Little Book to Washington
summary: McCullar contrasts the Georgia Bureau of Investigation, which she felt came to cover for the perpetrators, with the U.S. Department of Justice, whose investigators took statements and carried off the little notebook of meeting notes she never got back, leading to federal court in Americus over church burnings and the shooting at SNCC workers by a local police officer.

title: It Opened My Eyes
summary: Reflecting on Terrell County, McCullar says her parents protected her from working in white homes so she met little prejudice as a child, knowing only that white students had buses and new books, but working in the movement opened her eyes to ordering a hamburger through a hole in the wall and made her determined to make things better for everyone, poor whites included.

title: Then I Got Married and Moved Away
summary: McCullar recalls the canvassing slowing through 1963 and 1964 as people registered, work concentrating around campaigns to turn out voters, and her own departure to care for her sister's baby before marrying in 1967 and moving to Topeka, Kansas.

title: The Signs Were Down
topic: Coming Back to a Changed Area
summary: After Kansas and four years in Atlanta, McCullar returned to Albany to work as a CNA at Phoebe Putney hospital, finding the black-and-white signs and segregated water fountains gone, restaurants and theaters open, and Terrell County's schools integrated with everyone now riding the bus.

title: A Deputy Registrar for Seventeen Years
summary: McCullar resumed movement work with the NAACP and served as a deputy registrar from about 1979 until 1996, spending four hours each Saturday morning registering voters because people thought her dependable, and recalls the Terrell County NAACP's strength and the 1994 murder of branch president JL Barnes.

title: Register, and Then Vote
summary: McCullar reflects that things look better but much is hidden beneath smiles and handshakes, citing opposition she sees to the Black president, and closes by urging people to register and vote, to support and hold accountable the officials they put in office because if they do not, no one else will.
